title: Claude Certified Architect — Foundations
description: Master agent architecture, MCP integration, Claude Code, and production AI systems

lessons.title: Building Agent Loops
objectives: Implement a basic agent loop with tool calling
Handle stop_reason to control loop flow
Manage conversation state between API calls
lessons.concept: An agent loop is the core pattern for building autonomous AI systems. The loop reads the stop_reason from each Claude response: if it's 'tool_use', execute the tool and continue; if it's 'end_turn', present the final answer and stop.

examples.title: Basic Agent Loop
examples.explanation: This is the minimal agent loop. We check stop_reason after each response. Tool_use means Claude wants to call a tool; end_turn means Claude has finished.

examples.title: Agent Loop with Error Handling
examples.explanation: Add error handling for rate limits with exponential backoff, timeout handling, and iteration limits to prevent infinite loops.

examples.title: Streaming Agent Loop
examples.explanation: For better UX, stream responses as they arrive. Show thinking, text deltas, and detect when a tool call is about to happen.

examples.title: Parallel Tool Execution
examples.explanation: Claude can request multiple tools in one response. Execute them in parallel for maximum efficiency, then return all results together.

examples.title: Session-Based Agent Loop
examples.explanation: Sessions provide built-in conversation history management. Each turn is a separate API call but the session maintains the full context.

examples.title: Subagent Pattern
examples.explanation: A coordinator agent analyzes requests and delegates to specialized subagents. Each subagent handles a specific task type (research, coding, review).

examples.title: Tool Preconditions
examples.explanation: Use preconditions to enforce required tool sequences. For example, process_refund should only be called after get_customer and lookup_order.

examples.title: Confidence-Based Routing
examples.explanation: Route requests based on confidence scores. Low-confidence requests go to humans; high-complexity requests go to specialized agents.

exercises.prompt: Build an agent loop that calls a 'get_weather' tool and returns the weather for a user-provided city. Handle the tool_use and end_turn cases correctly.
hints: Use switch/case for different stop reasons
Append tool results to messages array

exercises.prompt: Add retry logic with exponential backoff to the agent loop. Retry up to 3 times on rate limit errors.
Implement a maximum iteration limit (e.g., 10 iterations) and stop gracefully if the limit is reached.

lab.title: Build a Customer Support Agent
lab.description: Create a customer support agent that can handle order status checks, refunds, and escalations. Use proper tool preconditions and error handling.
steps: Define tools: get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, escalate_to_human
Implement agent loop with proper stop_reason handling
Add tool preconditions (refund requires customer verification first)
Handle errors gracefully and escalate when needed
Add session persistence for multi-turn conversations

lessons.title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
lessons.concept: Multi-agent systems use specialized agents working together. A coordinator delegates tasks to subagents, aggregates results, and synthesizes final outputs.

examples.title: Coordinator Pattern
examples.explanation: The coordinator pattern has a main agent that analyzes requests, delegates to subagents, and synthesizes results. Subagents are specialized for specific tasks.

examples.title: Parallel Execution
examples.explanation: Run independent subagents in parallel. Web search and document analysis can happen simultaneously, then results are combined.

examples.title: Hierarchical Delegation
examples.explanation: For complex systems, use hierarchical delegation. A main coordinator delegates to domain coordinators, which in turn delegate to specialized agents.

examples.title: Context Passing
examples.explanation: Subagents need explicit context. Pass relevant files, requirements, and constraints directly in the prompt rather than expecting the subagent to infer.

examples.title: Error Propagation
examples.explanation: Subagents can fail. Handle errors gracefully: timeouts can return partial results, invalid outputs can be retried with corrections.

examples.title: Result Aggregation
examples.explanation: Subagents return results in different formats. Normalize outputs, group by topic, then synthesize into a coherent response.

examples.title: Subagent with Allowed Tools
examples.explanation: Limit subagent capabilities using allowedTools. This prevents subagents from making unintended changes.

examples.title: Fork Session for Isolation
examples.explanation: Use fork_session for exploration tasks. Brainstorming and exploration stay in an isolated context, and only the final decision returns to the main conversation.

exercises.prompt: Build a research pipeline with a coordinator, web search agent, and document analysis agent. Run them in parallel and aggregate results.
hints: Define structured output format for each agent

exercises.prompt: Add a timeout handler for subagents. If a subagent times out, return partial results and mark them accordingly.
Implement a supervisor agent that monitors subagent outputs and can request corrections or additional work.
hints: Can request re-run with different parameters

lab.title: Multi-Agent Research System
lab.description: Build a research pipeline where a coordinator delegates to web search, document analysis, and synthesis agents.
steps: Create coordinator that analyzes user query
Implement web search agent for online research
Implement document analysis agent for local files
Run agents in parallel and aggregate results
Add synthesis agent to produce final report

lessons.title: Tool Selection & Routing
lessons.concept: Claude chooses tools based on descriptions. Clear, differentiated descriptions are critical for reliable tool selection.

examples.title: Good Tool Descriptions
examples.explanation: Good descriptions include: what the tool does, when to use it, what it returns. Include trigger phrases like 'when users say X'.

examples.title: Differentiating Similar Tools
examples.explanation: Similar tools need different input field names and explicit differentiation in descriptions. Don't let both use 'query'.

examples.title: Handling Ambiguous Requests
examples.explanation: When get_customer returns multiple matches, don't guess. Return options and let Claude ask for clarification.

examples.title: Few-Shot Examples for Tool Selection
examples.code:
const systemPrompt = `
You are a customer support agent.

Examples of correct tool selection:


User: "Check my order #12345"
Thought: The user wants order status. They provided an order number, so use lookup_order.
Tool: lookup_order({"order_id": "12345"})

User: "I need help with my account"
Thought: The user wants account information. Use get_customer to verify their identity first.
Tool: get_customer({"identifier": <ask customer>})


User: "Refund for order #12345"
Thought: Need to verify customer and look up order before processing refund.
Tool: get_customer({"identifier": <customer info>})
You must call get_customer first to verify identity before any other action.`;
examples.explanation: Few-shot examples teach Claude the correct tool selection patterns for ambiguous scenarios.

examples.title: Preconditions as Code
examples.explanation: Programmatic preconditions enforce required sequences. The orchestrator tracks which tools have been called and blocks out-of-order calls.

examples.title: Routing Classifier
examples.explanation: A routing classifier analyzes incoming requests and routes to the appropriate specialized handler.

examples.title: Tool Descriptions for Edge Cases
examples.explanation: Edge cases belong in tool descriptions. Include MUST/SHOULD/never patterns, status constraints, and explicit exclusions.

examples.title: Dynamic Tool Selection
examples.explanation: Dynamic tool selection adds/removes tools based on context. VIP customers get additional tools; orders in certain states get refund options.

exercises.prompt: Write tool descriptions for a file operations system. Create tools for read, write, delete, and list. Make sure they are clearly differentiated.
hints: Specify input formats (e.g., file paths, glob patterns)

exercises.prompt: Add few-shot examples to a tool's system prompt showing correct and incorrect usage patterns.
hints: Show both correct and incorrect examples

exercises.prompt: Implement a precondition check that ensures get_customer is called before lookup_order.
hints: Track called tools in a Set

lab.title: Tool Selection Challenge
lab.description: Build a tool set for an e-commerce assistant and test tool selection with 20+ realistic user queries.
steps: Define 5 tools: search_products, get_customer, lookup_order, process_refund, track_shipment
Write detailed descriptions with trigger phrases
Fix any tool selection errors with improved descriptions or few-shot examples

lessons.title: Building MCP Servers
objectives: Create MCP server with tools and resources
lessons.concept: MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers expose tools and resources to Claude. Well-designed MCP servers provide clear interfaces, structured errors, and proper security.

examples.title: Basic MCP Server
examples.explanation: Basic MCP server structure. Tools return either success data or { isError: true, message } for errors.

examples.title: Structured Error Responses
examples.explanation: Structured errors include code, message, and retryable flag. This lets the agent handle errors appropriately.

examples.title: MCP Resources
examples.explanation: Resources provide persistent data that Claude can read. Use uriPattern for dynamic resources or resourceTemplate for collections.

examples.title: Tool with Multiple Parameters
examples.explanation: Complex tools can have multiple optional parameters with defaults, enums, and value constraints.

examples.title: Authentication in MCP
examples.explanation: Use middleware for authentication. The middleware runs before tool execution and can add context (like the authenticated user).

examples.title: Paginated Results
examples.explanation: For large result sets, use pagination. Return both the data and pagination metadata so Claude knows if there are more results.

examples.title: Batch Operations
examples.explanation: Batch operations let Claude update multiple items in one call. Return both successful and failed operations with details.

examples.title: MCP Server Configuration
examples.explanation: MCP servers are configured in .mcp.json with environment variable substitution. This keeps credentials out of version control.

exercises.prompt: Build an MCP server with a 'search_files' tool that accepts query, file_type, and max_results parameters. Return proper structured errors.
Add authentication middleware to the MCP server. Reject requests without valid Bearer tokens.
Create a paginated 'list_files' tool with page and per_page parameters.

lab.title: Build an MCP Server
lab.description: Create a full MCP server for a document management system with search, read, write, and delete tools.

lessons.title: CLAUDE.md & Project Configuration
lessons.concept: CLAUDE.md is the primary configuration file for Claude Code. Well-structured CLAUDE.md provides context, conventions, and constraints for the project.

examples.title: Basic CLAUDE.md Structure
examples.code:
# Project Overview

This is a REST API for managing tasks. Built with Express.js and PostgreSQL.

## Tech Stack
- Node.js 20+
- Express.js
- PostgreSQL with Prisma ORM
- Jest for testing

## Project Structure
```
/src
  /routes      - API route handlers
  /services   - Business logic
  /models     - Database models
  /middleware - Express middleware
```

## Key Conventions

### Error Handling
- All errors return { error: string, code: string }
- HTTP status codes: 400 bad input, 404 not found, 500 server error

### Response Format
- Success: { data: ..., meta: { ... } }
- Always include request ID for tracing
examples.explanation: CLAUDE.md should have clear sections: overview, tech stack, structure, and conventions. Claude reads this at project start.

examples.title: Path-Specific Rules
examples.code:
# Code Conventions by Path

## Frontend (src/client/**/*)
- Use functional components with hooks
- CSS modules for styling
- No class components

## Backend (src/server/**/*)
- Controller-Service-Repository pattern
- All async functions use try/catch
- Return typed responses

## Tests (*.test.ts)
- Unit tests for all services
- Integration tests for routes
- Mock external API calls
examples.explanation: Path-specific conventions let you define different rules for different parts of the codebase.

examples.title: .claude/rules/ Files
examples.code:
--- name: react-components description: React component conventions paths: - "src/components/**/*" - "src/hooks/**/*" --- # React Component Conventions ## File Structure ``` ComponentName/ index.tsx - Main component styles.ts - Styled components types.ts - TypeScript types test.tsx - Unit tests ``` ## Component Template ```tsx import { useState } from 'react'; export function ComponentName({ initialValue }: Props) { const [value, setValue] = useState(initialValue); return
{value}
; } ``` ## TypeScript - Always type props explicitly - Use interface, not type, for component props - Export all types
examples.explanation: .claude/rules/ files with YAML frontmatter let you define path-specific conventions. The paths field uses glob patterns.

examples.title: Custom Slash Commands
examples.code:
# .claude/commands/review.md

---
name: review
description: Run code review checklist
---

# Code Review Command

Review the selected code against this checklist:


## Correctness
- [ ] No obvious bugs or logic errors
- [ ] Edge cases handled
- [ ] Error handling present

## Security
- [ ] No hardcoded secrets
- [ ] Input validation
- [ ] SQL injection prevention

## Performance
- [ ] No N+1 queries
- [ ] Appropriate caching
- [ ] Index usage where needed

Provide a summary of issues found with severity (high/medium/low) and recommendations.
examples.explanation: Custom commands in .claude/commands/ are available as slash commands. Each command has metadata and detailed instructions.

examples.title: Skills with Context Isolation
examples.code:
---
name: explore-alternatives
description: Explore implementation alternatives
context: fork
allowed-tools:
  - Read
  - Grep
  - Bash
---

# Explore Alternatives Skill

This skill runs in an isolated fork session to explore different implementation approaches without polluting the main conversation.


## Usage
Run this skill when you need to evaluate multiple options before deciding on an implementation.

## Process
1. Understand the requirements
2. Research 2-3 alternative approaches
3. List pros and cons for each
4. Make a recommendation with justification

## Output Format
```
## Option A: [Name]
**Pros:** ...
**Cons:** ...



## Option B: [Name]
**Pros:** ...
**Cons:** ...

## Recommendation: [Option]
**Reasoning:** ...
```
examples.explanation: Skills can use context: fork to run in isolation. This prevents exploration context from affecting the main conversation.

examples.title: CLAUDE.md with Examples
examples.code:
## Response Formats

### List Items
When asked to list items, use this format:
```
1. **Item Name** - Brief description
2. **Item Name** - Brief description
```


### Error Messages
All error messages must include:
- What went wrong
- What the user should do
- Example of correct usage

### API Calls
When making API calls, include:
- Endpoint
- Request body
- Expected response format
examples.explanation: Include output format examples in CLAUDE.md. This ensures Claude generates responses in the format you expect.

examples.title: MCP Configuration in CLAUDE.md
examples.code:
## External Integrations

### Database
We use PostgreSQL with Prisma. MCP server provides:
- `query_database` - Execute read-only SQL
- `get_schema` - View table schemas

### APIs
- GitHub: Search PRs, check CI status
- Slack: Post notifications
- Linear: Create/update tickets

All MCP tools are configured in `.mcp.json`. See that file for credentials.
examples.explanation: Document external integrations in CLAUDE.md. Reference where credentials are stored and how each integration is used.

examples.title: CLAUDE.md Anti-Patterns
examples.code:
# What NOT to Put in CLAUDE.md

## ❌ Bad: Vague Instructions
"Be careful with the codebase"
"Follow best practices"

## ✅ Good: Specific Rules
"Never use `any` type in TypeScript"
"Always add error handling to async functions"
"Delete commented-out code before committing"


## ❌ Bad: Rules Without Context
"Use proper naming"

## ✅ Good: Naming Conventions
"Files: kebab-case (user-service.ts)
Classes: PascalCase (UserService)
Functions: camelCase (getUserById)
Constants: UPPER_SNAKE (MAX_RETRY_COUNT)"
examples.explanation: Avoid vague instructions. Be specific about what 'correct' means in your codebase.

exercises.prompt: Create a CLAUDE.md for a Node.js Express project. Include project structure, coding conventions, and response formats.
Create a .claude/rules/api-conventions.md file for API endpoints. Use YAML frontmatter with path patterns.
hints: Use paths array for glob patterns

exercises.prompt: Create a custom slash command for running tests.

lab.title: Configure a Real Project
lab.description: Configure Claude Code for an existing project with CLAUDE.md, rules, and custom commands.

lessons.title: JSON Schemas for Tool Output
objectives: Define JSON schemas with required/optional fields
lessons.concept: JSON schemas define the structure of tool inputs and outputs. Proper schemas ensure Claude generates valid, parseable responses.

examples.title: Basic JSON Schema
examples.explanation: Basic schema with required and optional fields. Required fields must be present; optional fields may be omitted.

examples.title: Nullable Fields
examples.explanation: Use ['type', 'null'] for nullable fields. This tells Claude the field can be present but empty.

examples.title: Enum Fields
examples.explanation: Enums restrict values to a specific set. Use when a field has limited valid values.

examples.title: Handling 'Other' Values
examples.explanation: When 'other' is a valid choice, require an additional detail field. Use if/then for conditional requirements.

examples.title: Validation Retry Loop
examples.explanation: Validation retry loops handle malformed output. On validation failure, provide the error and let Claude correct.

examples.title: Nested Objects
examples.explanation: Schemas can have nested objects and use $ref for reusable definitions. This creates complex but maintainable schemas.

examples.title: Array Validation
examples.explanation: Arrays can have min/max item counts. Each array item can be a complex object with its own validation.

examples.title: Strict Mode
examples.explanation: additionalProperties: false enables strict mode. Any unexpected fields cause validation failure.

exercises.prompt: Create a JSON schema for a product with name, price, description, category, and optional tags array.
Add validation with retry loop to a tool that extracts structured data.
hints: Retry on failure with error message

exercises.prompt: Create a schema with conditional required fields: if status is 'refunded', require refund_reason.
hints: Make refund_reason required only when status='refunded'

lab.title: Build a Data Extraction Pipeline
lab.description: Create a pipeline that extracts structured order data from unstructured text with validation.
steps: Define JSON schema for order extraction
Handle edge cases (missing fields, invalid formats)

lessons.title: Managing Context Windows
lessons.concept: Context windows have token limits. Effective context management keeps important information while reducing total tokens.

examples.title: Context Window Anatomy
examples.explanation: Context window includes system prompt, messages, tool definitions, and tool results. Budget tokens for each component.

examples.title: Progressive Summarization
examples.explanation: Progressive summarization compresses older conversation while keeping recent messages verbatim. Key facts (names, dates, decisions) are preserved.

examples.title: Fact Extraction
examples.explanation: Extract critical facts (names, numbers, commitments) into a structured block that persists across summarization. This prevents 'lost in the middle' for important details.

examples.title: Subagent for Discovery
examples.explanation: Verbose discovery tasks (searching many files) should run in subagents. Only the summary returns to main context.

examples.title: Scratchpad Files
examples.explanation: Scratchpad files hold intermediate state and decisions. Include the scratchpad in context so Claude maintains awareness of progress.

examples.title: Lost in the Middle
examples.explanation: Models are better at recalling info at the start or end of context. Structure prompts to keep critical info at these positions.

examples.title: Token Budgeting
examples.explanation: Calculate total tokens before making API calls. Fail fast if context would exceed limits rather than getting truncated results.

examples.title: Hybrid Memory Strategy
examples.explanation: Different info needs different storage. Recent conversation goes in context, facts are preserved, old stuff is summarized or retrieved.

exercises.prompt: Implement a token counter function and add context budget checking before API calls.
hints: Use tiktoken or similar for counting

exercises.prompt: Create a progressive summarization function that compresses old messages while keeping recent ones.
hints: Split messages into recent and older
Summarize older with key facts preservation

exercises.prompt: Build a fact extraction function that pulls critical info from verbose outputs.

lab.title: Context Management Challenge
lab.description: Handle a long conversation (100+ turns) while maintaining important context.
steps: Implement token counting and budget checking
